It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Left green at cols 9-11 needs to move RIGHT into left frame (cols 12-14). Right green at cols 30-32 needs to reach right frame (cols 45-47) but purple bar (cols 33-38) blocks it. Clicking green center selects it for arrow key movement.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 49 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see something important! In state 0, there's a white area at "rows=30-32, cols=30-32, pixels=9". This is a 3x3 white area at the same position as the right green's initial position! But wait, the right green is at rows 30-32, cols 27-29 in state 0, not cols 30-32.
Actually, looking more carefully, the white area at rows 30-32, cols 30-32 is a separate 3x3 white region. This might be the "target" position for the right green!
So the initial state has:
- Left green at cols 18-20, center=W
- Right green at cols 27-29, center=B
- White area at cols 30-32 (3x3)
- Left frame at cols 12-14
- Right frame at cols 45-47
The right green needs to move from cols 27-29 to cols 30-32 (the white area). But how?
Wait, but the right green is currently at cols 30-32, which is the white area. So it already reached the white area!
But the goal is to get the green into the frame. The right frame is at cols 45-47. So the right green needs to go from cols 30-32 to cols 45-47.
But the purple bar is at cols 33-38, blocking the path. And the right green is at cols 30-32, which is in the white area (rows 30-32, cols 30-32).
Wait, but the white area at cols 30-32 is only 3x3. The right green is 3x3. So the right green exactly fills this white area.
Now, the key question is: how do I move the right green from cols 30-32 to cols 45-47?
Looking at the state 0 -> 1 transition, the MOUSE(31, 28) action clicked on the right green's center. This swapped the centers (right: B->W, left: W->c). But it didn't move the right green.
Then in state 1 -> 2, the MOUSE(31, 19) action clicked on the left green's center. This swapped the centers again (left: c->W, right: W->c).
Then in state 2 -> 3, the LEFT action moved the left green from cols 18-20 to cols 15-17.
So the LEFT action moved the left green, not the right green. This suggests that the arrow keys move the green with the W center.
But wait, in state 2, the left green has center=W. So LEFT moved the left green (which has W center). This matches the hypothesis that arrow keys move the green with the W center.
Now, in state 36 (after my MOUSE action), the right green has center=W and the left green has center=c. So the arrow keys should move the right green (which has W center). But the UP actions didn't move the right green.
Why? Maybe the arrow keys only work horizontally (LEFT/RIGHT), not vertically (UP/DOWN)?
Let me test this by pressing RIGHT after clicking on the right green (which now has W center).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: right green at cols 30-32, center=W; left green at cols 15-17, center=c
# Try pressing RIGHT to see if it moves the right green (which has W center)
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
